title: Filling gaps in old timber floorboards
subtitle: Stopping floor draughts with flexible filler, papier-mache, and strip insert
excerpt: Gaps between old floorboards let cold air up from the sub-floor void. Flexible filler suits small gaps up to 3 mm; papier-mache mixed with wood glue works for wider gaps that flex with the boards; wood strip inserts are the best option for gaps over 8 mm.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. Historic England advice on filling gaps in suspended timber floors in traditional buildings. SPAB information sheet on floor draughts and appropriate repair.

glossaryTerms.definition: Filling or sealing gaps in the building envelope to stop air infiltration. For floors, this means filling board gaps with a material that tolerates seasonal movement without cracking or falling out.

content.text: A suspended timber ground floor has a ventilated void below it, kept dry by airbricks in the external wall. The same void that dries the joists also channels cold air up through gaps between the boards into the room above. In a house with a draughty floor, the combined heat loss through the floor and the infiltration through board gaps can be significant: a 20 m2 ground floor room in a 1930s semi with gaps averaging 5 mm across 25 board-lengths loses around 3-5% of the room's total heat through infiltration alone.

The gap-filling method depends on gap width. Never use a rigid filler such as sand-and-cement or plaster. Old boards move seasonally as humidity changes by 10-20% between summer and winter; a rigid fill cracks and falls out. You need a flexible, wood-compatible material.

Do not fill every airbrick gap or add draught sealing at the skirting boards before filling the floor gaps. The airbricks ventilate the sub-floor void to prevent dry rot in the joists. Seal the board gaps only. Leave the airbricks clear.

Lift the skirting boards to check for larger gaps at the perimeter. The edge boards often have a wider gap against the skirting than the boards have between each other. These edge gaps are usually the largest infiltration path.
Gaps up to 3 mm: flexible acrylic sealant

A paintable flexible acrylic sealant applied with a caulking gun fills small gaps neatly. Cut the nozzle to a tip smaller than the gap. Run a thin bead along the gap, then smooth with a wet finger. Remove excess immediately with a damp cloth. Let it set for 24 hours before walking on it.

Tear newspaper into small pieces and soak in water for 24 hours. Drain and squeeze out excess water. Mix with PVA wood glue to a thick paste (roughly 1 part PVA to 3 parts wet paper pulp by volume). Press firmly into the gap, slightly overfilling. When dry (allow 48 hours), sand flush with the board surface. The PVA makes the fill flexible enough to tolerate board movement. Add wood stain or floor paint if the appearance matters.
Gaps over 8 mm: wood strip inserts

Wide gaps need a solid insert. Cut a strip of pine, oak, or similar hardwood to a width fractionally wider than the gap (use a table saw or have a timber merchant rip it for you). Plane or sand the strip to a slight taper: wider at the top than the bottom. Apply PVA glue to both sides of the strip and tap it into the gap with a mallet and offcut block, tapered side down. The taper wedges the strip as it descends and the PVA locks it. Once set, plane or sand flush.
The perimeter gap at the skirting

The gap between the last board and the skirting board is usually the largest in the floor. If the skirting is removable, take it off, fill the gap with a flexible sealant or papier-mache depending on width, and refix the skirting. If the skirting cannot be removed, apply a flexible sealant into the visible gap at the skirting line. Either way, do not seal the gap with a rigid filler.

Close all doors and windows in the room. Hold a hand near the floor on a cold day and move slowly across the boards. Draughts from filled gaps should be gone. Alternatively, light a stick of incense and hold it near each gap: smoke deflected horizontally shows airflow. The test for success is no deflection.
the floor is a fast-payback measure. Total materials cost is under £20 and payback on the gas saving is inside one heating season. The labour is a weekend afternoon project.
